title: How to Make Single-Origin Chocolate at Home: A Step-by-Step Guide

hero.alt: Artisan chocolate on a wooden board
hero.tagline: From raw cacao beans to your first handmade chocolate bar
hero.caption: Handcrafted single-origin chocolate ready for tasting

blocks.text: This guide assumes you have **raw cacao beans** and a **home oven or roaster**. You can source beans online from specialty suppliers — try searching for **single-origin cacao** from Peru, Madagascar, or Tanzania.

blocks.content: Making chocolate from bean to bar at home is easier than you think. The process takes patience, but the result is far more rewarding than anything you can buy off the shelf (fn:1). Here's what you'll need before you start.

items: A baking sheet and oven (or a home coffee roaster)
A molinillo or high-speed blender (Vitamix works well)
A cocoa press or fine-mesh strainer (optional but helpful)

blocks.content: Preheat your oven to 275°F (135°C). Spread the cacao beans in a single layer on a baking sheet. Roast for 15-25 minutes, shaking the pan halfway through (fn:2). The beans are done when the shells have darkened and you can smell a rich chocolate aroma. Let them cool completely.

blocks.code:
Temperature: 275°F
Time: 20 minutes
Bean origin: Peru, Marañón Canyon
Notes: Fruity aroma, shells cracking at 14 min

blocks.text: **Roast level matters.** A lighter roast preserves fruity and floral notes. A darker roast develops deeper chocolate flavor but reduces acidity. Start light and experiment.

blocks.content: Once cooled, crack the beans gently with a rolling pin or in a cloth bag. The goal is to separate the shells (husks) from the nibs (the inner meat). Winnowing means removing the light shells from the heavier nibs — the easiest way is to use a hairdryer and a bowl, letting the breeze carry the shells away while the nibs stay put (fn:3).

This step is the most labor-intensive but also the most satisfying. You'll end up with a bowl of pure cacao nibs — the foundation of your chocolate. Discard or compost the shells (or steep them as cacao tea, which is delicious).

blocks.text: **Watch for shell fragments.** Even small pieces can make your chocolate gritty. Take your time and pick out any obvious shells by hand after winnowing. The texture of your final chocolate depends on it.

blocks.content: This is where the magic happens. Add your cacao nibs to a high-speed blender or a dedicated molinillo. Grind continuously for 20-30 minutes (fn:4). The heat from friction will melt the cacao butter, transforming the nibs from a dry powder into a liquid paste called **cacao liquor**. Be patient — this takes time.

blocks.text: **Speed matters.** Start slow to avoid overheating the blender, then gradually increase. If the mixture gets too hot (above 50°C), pause and let it cool. Heat destroys delicate flavor compounds.

blocks.content: Tempering is the most technically demanding step. It involves carefully heating and cooling the chocolate to specific temperatures so that the cocoa butter crystallizes in a stable form (fn:5). Properly tempered chocolate has a glossy shine, a clean snap, and doesn't bloom (develop white streaks) over time.

Pour the tempered chocolate into your silicone molds. Tap the molds gently on the counter to release air bubbles. Let the chocolate set at room temperature (18-20°C) for 2-4 hours. Do NOT refrigerate — rapid cooling produces unstable crystals and ruins the temper.

blocks.code:
# Check temper readiness
# Dip a knife tip into the chocolate
# If it sets glossy within 3 minutes at room temp, you're ready!

# Mold and wait
pour chocolate into mold
tap to release bubbles
wait 3 hours at 20°C
demold gently

blocks.text: **Setting time varies** by room temperature and chocolate thickness. Thin bars set faster than thick blocks. If your chocolate doesn't set after 4 hours, the temper may have failed — but don't worry, you can re-temper it!

blocks.content: Congratulations! You've made chocolate from bean to bar. Let it rest for 24-48 hours at room temperature before tasting — this allows the flavor to fully develop and the crystal structure to stabilize. Then snap off a piece, listen for that clean break, and savor every note of the origin you chose.

footnotes.text: Homemade chocolate has no preservatives, no emulsifiers, and no added vanilla or lecithin unless you choose to add them. Pure cacao, pure flavor.
Roasting time depends on bean size and moisture content. Peruvian beans tend to roast faster than larger Madagascar beans. Trust your nose.
A salad spinner also works for winnowing — spin gently and the shells fly up while the nibs stay at the bottom. Ingenious hack from home chocolate-making communities.
A molinillo (Mexican stone grinder) is traditional and produces the smoothest chocolate. A high-speed blender works but may require scraping down the sides frequently.
The key temperatures for dark chocolate tempering: melt to 45°C, cool to 27°C, warm to 31°C. This creates stable beta crystals — the hallmark of professional chocolate.
